title: Farmhouse Table — No Pocket Holes
summary: A full-size farmhouse table with breadboard ends, joined entirely with notched legs, glue, and screws — the design that assumes you don't own a pocket hole jig.
description:
Most farmhouse table plans quietly require a pocket hole jig. This one is built around not having one: the legs are notched to receive the aprons and stretcher, so the frame's strength comes from wood bearing on wood, with screws just clamping the joint shut. Each leg is actually two 2x4s — an outside leg and an inside leg, each notched differently, screwed face-to-face — which gets you the look and strength of a chunky solid leg while every notch is cut in cheap, manageable 2x4 stock.

The notches ARE the build. Cut them carefully — saw kerfs to depth and chisel or snap out the waste, or nibble with repeated passes — and the rest of the table assembles like a kit. Cut them sloppy and every joint wobbles in its seat forever. Take the time on the first leg to develop the technique; the other seven notches go fast once you have it.

The top is seven 2x6s with 2x8 breadboard ends, screwed down from above in a deliberate pattern (1" from each end and edge, one center screw) and pulled tight to 2x2 under-supports from below. One honest caveat this design carries: the breadboard ends are screwed rigidly across the grain of a 38"-wide top, so expect some seasonal gapping at that joint — construction lumber moves. It's a farmhouse table; the small gaps are part of the vocabulary. A note in the original plan even offers rotating the leg assemblies to expose the notch detail, Restoration-Hardware style, at the cost of end-chair legroom — decide before step 6, not after.

tools.note: Crosscuts AND the notch kerfs — set the depth and make repeated passes, then clean out the waste.
The clean way to pare the notch bottoms flat after kerfing. A sharp 3/4" chisel is plenty.
Predrilling is not optional on the tabletop ends — 2x6 ends split.
Pilot bits for the tabletop screw pattern.
Persuading notch waste out, and seating joints.
Every notch shoulder and the tabletop screw pattern get marked before anything gets cut.
Construction lumber to dining table is mostly a sanding journey. Start coarse.

materials.name: 2x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Legs, aprons, stretcher. Pick the straightest boards in the store — a bowed leg can't be fixed later.

materials.name: 2x6, 8 ft
materials.note: The tabletop. Choose boards with matching grain and no crown.

materials.name: Self-tapping wood screws, #9 x 2-3/4", star drive
Self-tapping wood screws, #10 x 4", star drive
Wood glue
materials.note: Every joint gets glue — the screws clamp, the glue holds.

materials.name: Stain and topcoat
materials.note: A dining top wants a durable film finish — polyurethane, not just wax.

cutList.note: Notched top AND bottom — different from the outside legs.
Carry the stretcher between the leg pairs.
Add 4" if you rotate the legs for the exposed-notch look.

steps.title: Notch the outside legs
steps.body:
Mark the notches on the four outside legs (A) per the plan diagrams. To cut each one: set the circular saw to the notch depth, make repeated kerf passes inside the marked shoulders about 1/4" apart, knock the waste fingers out with a hammer, and pare the bottom flat with a chisel.

Go slow on the first one — it's technique practice for the seven that follow. The joint only seats solidly if the notch bottom is flat.

steps.title: Notch the inside legs — top and bottom
steps.body:
The inside legs (B) get notches at BOTH ends: bottom notches to receive the end supports, top notches for the aprons. Mark them from the diagrams, double-check against an outside leg before cutting, and cut with the same kerf-and-chisel method.

The top and bottom notches are different — label each leg's orientation in pencil the moment it's cut.

steps.title: Laminate the leg pairs
steps.body: Glue and screw each inside leg to its outside leg with 2-3/4" screws, driving from the inside leg so the screw heads end up hidden. Placement matters: the legs are handed, and which side the inside leg sits on determines where its notches face. Dry-fit all four assemblies in table position before driving a single screw.
materials: Self-tapping wood screws, #9 x 2-3/4", star drive

steps.title: Connect the leg pairs with the end supports
steps.body:
Seat the bottom end supports (C) in their notches and screw them to the legs — two screws into the inside leg, two into the outside leg, per side.

Decision point from the plan: rotating the leg assemblies puts this support (and its notch detail) on the OUTSIDE, like the Restoration Hardware original — but costs end-chair legroom and adds 4" to the stretcher. Either is right; pick now.
materials: Self-tapping wood screws, #9 x 2-3/4", star drive

steps.title: Install the stretcher
steps.body: Drop the stretcher (D) into the end supports' notches and screw it down. The base is now a freestanding H — stand it on a flat surface and check nothing rocks before moving on.
materials: Self-tapping wood screws, #9 x 2-3/4", star drive

steps.title: Build the apron frame
steps.body: On a level surface, screw the side aprons (E) and end aprons (F) into a rectangle. Check the diagonals — this frame decides whether the tabletop sits square on the base.
materials: Self-tapping wood screws, #9 x 2-3/4", star drive

steps.title: Marry the apron to the base
steps.body: Fit the apron frame down into the top notches of the legs. Drive the 4" screws at an angle through the apron into each leg — three screws per leg, and be generous with glue in the notch seats. Then check the whole table for square one more time; this is the last moment it's adjustable.
materials: Self-tapping wood screws, #10 x 4", star drive

steps.title: Add the under-top supports
steps.body: Mark the side aprons every 5-3/4" and screw the 2x2 supports (I) across between them with 2-3/4" screws. The spacing doesn't need to be exact and the last bay will be narrower — their job is to give every tabletop board something to be pulled down against.
materials: Self-tapping wood screws, #9 x 2-3/4", star drive

steps.title: Add the overhang supports
steps.body: Screw the overhang supports (G, H) around the apron's top edge, flush with the top, using 2-3/4" screws — and screw these in thoroughly. They're what the tabletop's overhang hangs from; a lifting overhang is this exact part under-fastened.
materials: Self-tapping wood screws, #9 x 2-3/4", star drive

steps.title: Fit the breadboard ends
steps.body: Position the 2x8 breadboard ends (J) per the plan's measurements — they cap the tabletop boards and overhang the end aprons. Screw through with the 4" screws into the legs, then up from the underside of the end overhang supports into the breadboards.
materials: Self-tapping wood screws, #10 x 4", star drive

steps.title: Lay the tabletop, center board first
steps.body:
Mark the screw pattern on both ends of every tabletop board (K): 1" from the end, 1" from each edge, one in the middle — and PREDRILL every mark, because a screw 1" from the end of a 2x6 without a pilot hole is a split, guaranteed.

Set the center board first, screw its ends down through the top, then pull it tight from below with screws up through the under-supports (I).
materials: Self-tapping wood screws, #9 x 2-3/4", star drive

steps.title: Work outward, then lock the ends
steps.body:
Add the remaining tabletop boards from the center out, clamping each tight to its neighbor before screwing — the top should read as one surface, not seven boards. Screw down through the marked ends and up from the under-supports as you go.

Finally, drive the 4" screws through the tabletop boards into the breadboard ends. Sand the whole top flat, starting coarse, and finish with something that shrugs off spilled milk — this is the most-touched surface in a house.
materials: Self-tapping wood screws, #10 x 4", star drive

images.alt: Farmhouse dining table with thick laminated legs and breadboard-end plank top
